extracted_rules.resource: Right to give orders and receive obedience
proof: [§1] ...the wise man should give orders, not receive them; nor should he obey others, but the less wise should obey him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly states that higher intellectual status (Wisdom) confers a legal or customary right to command others, while the 'less wise' are traditionally expected to obey.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to a broad class of people categorized by intellectual capacity, which in Aristotelian thought often correlated with the ruling class.

extracted_rules.resource: Exemption from labor for mathematical study
proof: [§1] Thus the mathematical sciences originated in the neighborhood of Egypt, because there the priestly class was allowed leisure.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific social group (Egyptian priests) who were systematically granted freedom from the necessity of labor (leisure) to pursue abstract sciences.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This refers to a specific sub-group (priests) in a specific geographical location (Egypt).

extracted_rules.resource: Recognition of superior professional wisdom
proof: [§1] For the same reason we consider that the master craftsmen in every profession are more estimable and know more and are wiser than the artisans, because they know the reasons of the things which are done
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a societal stratification within professions where master craftsmen are granted higher esteem and recognized as having greater knowledge compared to manual artisans.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a significant portion of the urban skilled labor force.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be viewed as conscious agents
proof: [§1] ...but we think that the artisans, like certain inanimate objects, do things, but without knowing what they are doing (as, for instance, fire burns); only whereas inanimate objects perform all their actions in virtue of a certain natural quality, artisans perform theirs through habit.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Artisans are likened to inanimate objects, stripping them of intellectual agency in the hierarchy of wisdom and professional standing.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Artisans represent a broad category of manual laborers in Greek society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to absolute independence of knowledge
proof: [§1] For this reason its acquisition might justly be supposed to be beyond human power, since in many respects human nature is servile
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle notes that human nature itself is considered 'servile' in the context of possessing divine knowledge, implying a lack of total independence or autonomy in the pursuit of the highest sciences.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the entire human race.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to leisure for intellectual pursuits
proof: [§18] Hence when all the discoveries of this kind were fully developed, the sciences which relate neither to pleasure nor yet to the necessities of life were invented, and first in those places where men had leisure. Thus the mathematical sciences originated in the neighborhood of Egypt, because there the priestly class was allowed leisure.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies a specific group (the Egyptian priesthood) that was legally or customarily granted 'leisure'—exemption from manual labor or the search for necessities—which allowed them to develop speculative sciences like mathematics. This is a concrete social arrangement regarding how a group's time and resources are managed.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule is specific to a particular occupation (priests) within a specific geographic context mentioned by the author to explain the origins of a science.

extracted_rules.resource: Recognition as possessors of wisdom
proof: [§13] For the same reason we consider that the master craftsmen in every profession are more estimable and know more and are wiser than the artisans, because they know the reasons of the things which are done; but we think that the artisans, like certain inanimate objects, do things, but without knowing what they are doing (as, for instance, fire burns); [§14] only whereas inanimate objects perform all their actions in virtue of a certain natural quality, artisans perform theirs through habit.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a societal stratification where 'artisans' (manual laborers/handicraftsmen) are categorized alongside 'inanimate objects' and denied the status of 'wise' or 'knowing.' This distinction determines who is considered 'estimable' (worthy of high social/legal regard) based on their occupation and the nature of their labor.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Artisans represent a broad and significant segment of the free and semi-free population in ancient urban centers.

extracted_rules.resource: Professional estimability and social wisdom
proof: [§13] For the same reason we consider that the master craftsmen in every profession are more estimable and know more and are wiser than the artisans, because they know the reasons of the things which are done; [§14] Thus the master craftsmen are superior in wisdom, not because they can do things, but because they possess a theory and know the causes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly elevates master craftsmen over manual artisans. This is not just a personal opinion but a description of how these roles are 'considered' and 'held' in society, affecting their social standing and authority within their professions.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This refers to the upper tier of the labor and professional hierarchy.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to exercise command and social authority
proof: [§23] Again among the sciences we consider that that science which is desirable in itself and for the sake of knowledge is more nearly Wisdom than that which is desirable for its results, and that the superior is more nearly Wisdom than the subsidiary; for the wise man should give orders, not receive them; nor should he obey others, but the less wise should obey him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a societal norm or 'opinion we hold' regarding the hierarchy of wisdom, stating that the possession of high-level knowledge (Wisdom/Metaphysics) entitles a person to command others rather than obey them.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to a small elite distinguished by intellectual achievement (the wise).

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from the orders of others
proof: [§23] Again among the sciences we consider that that science which is desirable in itself and for the sake of knowledge is more nearly Wisdom than that which is desirable for its results, and that the superior is more nearly Wisdom than the subsidiary; for the wise man should give orders, not receive them; nor should he obey others, but the less wise should obey him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that those with less wisdom should obey those with greater wisdom, establishing a rule of social subordination based on intellectual status.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes a broad social stratification where the majority (the less wise) are expected to obey an intellectual elite.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to independent professional decision-making
proof: [§27] And that science is supreme, and superior to the subsidiary, which knows for what end each action is to be done; i.e. the Good in each particular case, and in general the highest Good in the whole of nature.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle establishes a hierarchy of occupations/sciences where 'subsidiary' fields are structurally inferior to 'supreme' sciences that determine the 'end' or purpose of actions. This translates to the practitioners of the master science directing the practitioners of the subsidiary ones.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to specific categories of professional and intellectual classes.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to philosophical education and speculation
proof: [§31] The actual course of events bears witness to this; for speculation of this kind began with a view to recreation and pastime, at a time when practically all the necessities of life were already supplied.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle notes that the pursuit of wisdom for its own sake only occurs once the 'necessities of life' are secured. This creates an implicit rule where those burdened by the need for labor to meet biological necessities (the poor) are excluded from the resource of higher speculation.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This affects a large portion of the population who are not part of the leisure class.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be included in intellectual history/lineage
proof: [§44] Whether this view of the primary entity is really ancient and time-honored may perhaps be considered uncertain; however, it is said that this was Thales’ opinion concerning the first cause. (I say nothing of Hippo, because no one would presume to include him in this company, in view of the paltriness of his intelligence.)
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly excludes Hippo from the 'company' of recognized philosophers and thinkers not based on his lack of ideas, but because of a perceived innate trait ('paltriness of his intelligence'). This indicates a customary rule in Greek intellectual discourse where those deemed naturally low in intelligence are denied the resource of being cited or treated as part of the historical/intellectual record.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule is highly specific as it targets a specific named individual (Hippo) based on a trait, though it implies a broader exclusion for anyone falling into the category of 'paltry intelligence'.

extracted_rules.resource: Science of combat and purposeful action
proof: [§59] They are like untrained soldiers in a battle, who rush about and often strike good blows, but without science; in the same way these thinkers do not seem to understand their own statements, since it is clear that upon the whole they seldom or never apply them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle uses the figure of 'untrained soldiers' as a baseline for ineffective and accidental action in a real-world military context. This implies a societal rule or norm where untrained participants in a battle lack the 'science' or systematic knowledge required for deliberate effectiveness, as opposed to trained ones.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a broad class of individuals (soldiers) during a standard societal state (warfare).

extracted_rules.resource: Association with limiting and positive cosmic principles
proof: [§74] Others of this same school hold that there are ten principles, which they enunciate in a series of corresponding pairs: (1.) Limit and the Unlimited; (2.) Odd and Even; (3.) Unity and Plurality; (4.) Right and Left; (5.) Male and Female; (6.) Rest and Motion; (7.) Straight and Crooked; (8.) Light and Darkness; (9.) Good and Evil; (10.) Square and Oblong.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In Pythagorean thought as record by Aristotle, the 'Male' is categorized within a column of positive/ordered principles (Limit, Odd, Unity, Right, Light, Good), granting the group a superior metaphysical standing compared to the 'Female' which is aligned with the 'Unlimited' and 'Evil'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule outlines a fundamental gender-based stratification that applies to the entire population as a metaphysical and social justification for hierarchy.

extracted_rules.resource: Association with limiting and positive cosmic principles
proof: [§74] Others of this same school hold that there are ten principles, which they enunciate in a series of corresponding pairs: (1.) Limit and the Unlimited; (2.) Odd and Even; (3.) Unity and Plurality; (4.) Right and Left; (5.) Male and Female; (6.) Rest and Motion; (7.) Straight and Crooked; (8.) Light and Darkness; (9.) Good and Evil; (10.) Square and Oblong.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text places 'Female' in a linguistic and conceptual series with 'the Unlimited', 'Plurality', 'Left', 'Darkness', and 'Evil'. This represents a loss of cosmic/metaphysical standing relative to the male.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: As a binary classification, this affects half the population and serves as a broad social-cultural rule.

extracted_rules.resource: Capacity for multiple simultaneous impregnations
proof: [§86] Such too is the relation of male to female: the female is impregnated in one coition, but one male can impregnate many females.
[§94] Such too is the relation of male to female: the female is impregnated in one coition, but one male can impregnate many females.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle uses a biological observation about human reproduction as a definitive analogue for metaphysical principles of form and matter. By framing the male's capacity to impregnate many as a rule of nature comparable to a craftsman making many tables from one form, he reinforces a social-biological hierarchy where the male possesses a multiplicative power that the female lacks.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule describes a general biological/social fact attributed to all men in the society relative to all women.

extracted_rules.resource: Capacity for multiple simultaneous impregnations
proof: [§86] Such too is the relation of male to female: the female is impregnated in one coition, but one male can impregnate many females.
[§94] Such too is the relation of male to female: the female is impregnated in one coition, but one male can impregnate many females.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly limits the female's reproductive role to a single event (one coition) while contrasting it with the male's expansive capacity. In the context of Aristotelian thought, these biological 'rules' ground the legal and customary status of women as passive recipients of form (matter) rather than active providers of it.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the entire category of women within the described natural and social order.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to define the whole of reality
proof: [§120] However, these thinkers are really concerned only with the theories of generation and destruction and motion (for in general it is only with reference to this aspect of reality that they look for their principles and causes).

[§121] Those, however, who make their study cover the whole of reality, and who distinguish between sensible and non-sensible objects, clearly give their attention to both kinds; hence in their case we may consider at greater length what contributions, valuable or otherwise, they make to the inquiry which is now before us.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle distinguishes between the 'physicists' (early monists/materialists) and a more elevated class of thinkers. He limits the scope and authority of the physicists' intellectual contributions to the sensible world of motion, while granting the 'whole of reality'—including the non-sensible—to a different group (Platonic/Pythagorean thinkers), effectively marginalizing the physicists' standing in the inquiry into 'the whole'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific professional and intellectual class (philosophers and scientists) within Greek society but defines their standing in the most prestigious discursive spaces.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to learn through prior knowledge
proof: [§130] But all learning proceeds, wholly or in part, from what is already known; whether it is through demonstration or through definition—since the parts of the definition must be already known and familiar.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a fundamental pedagogical rule of his society: that all formal learning (demonstration or definition) is legally and customarily structured to depend on pre-existing knowledge. This acts as a procedural requirement for anyone attempting to participate in the sciences or education.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general epistemological and educational rule applying to any student or person engaged in the process of learning within Greek intellectual life.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to utilize points in formal proofs
proof: [§130] Plato steadily rejected this class of objects as a geometrical fiction, but he recognized the beginning of a line, and he frequently assumed this latter class, i.e. the indivisible lines.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle notes a specific restrictive rule in the Platonic school of thought where the use of 'points' as a valid category of object was rejected (barred) in favor of 'indivisible lines,' effectively restricting the conceptual resources available to practitioners of that discipline.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to a subset of intellectuals and mathematicians following Platonic doctrine.

extracted_rules.resource: Exemption from prior specialized knowledge
proof: [§130] because just as a man who is beginning to learn geometry can have previous knowledge of other facts, but no previous knowledge of the principles of that science or of the things about which he is to learn, so it is in the case of all other branches of knowledge.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a standard social and intellectual rule that a beginner in a specific craft or science is not expected to already possess the internal principles of that science to begin their education, granting them access to the discipline despite their lack of initial expertise.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any individual beginning a new trade, craft, or science in the society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in learning without prior knowledge
proof: [§163] But all learning proceeds, wholly or in part, from what is already known; whether it is through demonstration or through definition—since the parts of the definition must be already known and familiar. The same is true of induction.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a fundamental epistemic and social rule of Greek education: learning is not a start-from-zero process but requires the learner to already possess specific conceptual foundations. This acts as a barrier or requirement for anyone seeking to enter a branch of knowledge.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general rule governing the process of education and intellectual participation for any student or learner in this society.

extracted_rules.resource: Previous knowledge of foundational principles
proof: [§162] ...just as a man who is beginning to learn geometry can have previous knowledge of other facts, but no previous knowledge of the principles of that science or of the things about which he is to learn, so it is in the case of all other branches of knowledge.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific group (beginners in a discipline) and notes their necessary lack of access to the foundational principles of that specific science prior to the start of their instruction.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: It applies specifically to the early stages of professional or academic training (novices).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to gain specific sensory knowledge
proof: [§166] Further, how can one gain knowledge of the objects of a particular sense-perception without possessing that sense?
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle frames the possession of a physical sense as an absolute prerequisite for certain types of knowledge. While phrased as a rhetorical question, it functions as a rule of human capacity and societal understanding regarding who can claim or possess certain types of expertise or testimony.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any individual lacking one of the five senses in relation to the specific field of knowledge associated with that sense.

extracted_rules.resource: Intellectual accessibility of arguments
proof: [§172] The effect of a lecture depends upon the habits of the listener; because we expect the language to which we are accustomed, and anything beyond this seems not to be on the same level, but somewhat strange and unintelligible on account of its unfamiliarity; for it is the familiar that is intelligible.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a cognitive rule where the resource (intelligibility/information) is gated by the listener's habits and past training. If the speaker uses a method unfamiliar to the listener, the listener loses access to the knowledge being presented.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies generally to any person engaged in learning or attending lectures in Greek society.

extracted_rules.resource: Rational judgment in law
proof: [§172] The powerful effect of familiarity is clearly shown by the laws, in which the fanciful and puerile survivals prevail, through force of habit, against our recognition of them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly notes that in his society, legal systems are governed by habit and 'puerile' traditions that override rational recognition. This represents a loss of the resource of 'rational legal principles' in favor of customary 'survivals.'
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes the functioning of the legal system as a whole for all citizens.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to simultaneous pursuit of method and knowledge
proof: [§172] Hence one must have been already trained how to take each kind of argument, because it is absurd to seek simultaneously for knowledge and for the method of obtaining it; and neither is easy to acquire.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule for the acquisition of knowledge: the resource (the method of obtaining knowledge) must be acquired *before* the knowledge itself. One is barred from seeking both at once, imposing a chronological restriction on intellectual access.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the specific subset of the population engaged in higher education or philosophical inquiry.

extracted_rules.resource: Use of mathematical accuracy in discourse
proof: [§172] Mathematical accuracy is not to be demanded in everything, but only in things which do not contain matter. Hence this method is not that of natural science, because presumably all nature is concerned with matter.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle defines a rule of inquiry: the resource (mathematical accuracy) is explicitly withheld from the field of natural science. Those practicing natural science are prohibited from using this specific argumentative resource because of the nature of their subject matter.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule specifically targets practitioners of natural science (physikoi).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to information tailored to personal habits
proof: [§195] The effect of a lecture depends upon the habits of the listener; because we expect the language to which we are accustomed, and anything beyond this seems not to be on the same level, but somewhat strange and unintelligible on account of its unfamiliarity; for it is the familiar that is intelligible.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a social and pedagogical reality where the effectiveness of communication is governed by the pre-existing habits of the audience. He acknowledges that listeners possess a standard of intelligibility based on their specific background (mathematical, poetic, or literal), which dictates how knowledge must be presented to them.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies broadly to any person in an audience or educational setting in Greek society.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection of traditional customary laws
proof: [§196] The powerful effect of familiarity is clearly shown by the laws, in which the fanciful and puerile survivals prevail, through force of habit, against our recognition of them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a societal rule where 'fanciful and puerile' legal remnants are maintained and enforced simply because they are familiar to the citizenry. This indicates that long-standing custom grants a resource of legal stability/continuity to the group, even when the laws are antiquated.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Laws apply to the entire body of citizens within a state.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to advanced scientific instruction
proof: [§198] Hence one must have been already trained how to take each kind of argument, because it is absurd to seek simultaneously for knowledge and for the method of obtaining it; and neither is easy to acquire.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle posits a rule of intellectual access: one cannot access 'knowledge' (the subject matter) unless they have previously acquired 'the method.' This establishes a sequence of access where advanced resources are restricted to those who have already undergone preliminary training.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule specifically targets the subset of the population engaged in formal education or philosophical inquiry.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to demand mathematical certainty
proof: [§198] Mathematical accuracy is not to be demanded in everything, but only in things which do not contain matter. [§199] Hence this method is not that of natural science, because presumably all nature is concerned with matter.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines a methodological restriction for those practicing natural science (physikoi). They are denied the resource of 'mathematical accuracy' in their proofs because the material nature of their subject makes such a standard inapplicable in the Greek intellectual tradition.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific professional/intellectual class: those studying nature and matter.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to judge universal truth and error
proof: [§200] And whose province will it be, if not the philosopher’s, to study truth and error with respect to them?
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle asserts that the philosopher has a unique and authoritative role in judging the truth and error of universal axioms, comparing the process of hearing conflicting theories to a judge hearing both sides in a lawsuit.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to those defined as philosophers or seekers of 'Wisdom' within the intellectual hierarchy.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to contradict sovereign knowledge
proof: [§200] Inasmuch as Wisdom is the most sovereign and authoritative kind of knowledge, which the other sciences, like slaves, may not contradict, the knowledge of the end and of the Good resembles Wisdom
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly subordinates other sciences to 'Wisdom' (metaphysics/philosophy), using the analogy of slaves to indicate they lack the right to challenge or contradict the primary science.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While 'scientists' is a broad category, the rule specifically targets those practicing 'other' (subordinate) sciences relative to the 'sovereign' science.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to high-status intellectual demonstration
proof: [§200] And so for this reason some of the sophists, e.g. Aristippus, spurned mathematics, on the ground that in the other arts, even the mechanical ones such as carpentry and cobbling, all explanation is of the kind because it is better or worse, while mathematics takes no account of good and bad.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies 'mechanical' arts like carpentry and cobbling as distinct from and lower than theoretical sciences like mathematics or philosophy, noting they rely on 'better or worse' (utilitarian) explanations rather than higher demonstrative principles.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This refers to the large class of manual laborers and craftsmen in Greek society.

extracted_rules.resource: Status as most learned investigator
proof: [§200] or whom of the masters of these sciences shall we consider to be most learned in the subject which we are investigating?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text recognizes a specific status ('master') for those who head a science, granting them the standing of 'most learned' and the authority to be the primary subjects of investigation.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a very small elite of intellectual leaders/heads of schools.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to a hearing of both sides
proof: [§205] Further, one who has heard all the conflicting theories, like one who has heard both sides in a lawsuit, is necessarily more competent to judge.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle uses the legal requirement of hearing both sides in a court case as a familiar, established social norm to illustrate his philosophical method. This confirms that in Athenian legal practice, a judge or jury was expected to grant both parties the right to present their case for a competent judgment to be rendered.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to anyone involved in a legal dispute, which is a significant but specific portion of the citizenry.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to contradict sovereign knowledge
proof: [§224] Inasmuch as Wisdom is the most sovereign and authoritative kind of knowledge, which the other sciences, like slaves, may not contradict, the knowledge of the end and of the Good resembles Wisdom (since everything else is for the sake of the end )
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a hierarchical relationship between different fields of knowledge, explicitly using the metaphor of slavery to define the lack of autonomy for 'lesser' sciences. In this intellectual hierarchy, practitioners of specific/subordinate sciences are barred from contradicting the principles of the 'sovereign' science (Wisdom/Philosophy), mirroring the social lack of agency of slaves.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a broad but specific segment of the population involved in specialized intellectual or technical labor, contrasted with the 'philosopher' or master of 'Wisdom'.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to study and judge universal first principles
proof: [§232] The axioms are most universal, and are the first principles of everything. And whose province will it be, if not the philosopher’s, to study truth and error with respect to them?
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies a specific group—philosophers—as the rightful possessors of the authority to investigate the most universal axioms and principles of all things. This grants them a unique intellectual and professional jurisdiction over the foundations of all other sciences.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to a very small, specialized subset of the population (philosophers) regarding their specific professional domain.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to scientific knowledge via species and genera
proof: [§252] And if to gain scientific knowledge of things is to gain it of the species after which things are named, the genera are first principles of the species.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that the path to 'scientific knowledge' (a concrete social and intellectual status in Greek society) is mediated through specific logical categories (species and genera). Only those utilizing these principles can claim the status of having 'scientific knowledge'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While broader than just philosophers, this applies to the educated/scientific class specifically.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from the need for nourishment
proof: [§261] For they make the first principles Gods or generated from Gods, and say that whatever did not taste of the nectar and ambrosia became mortal—clearly using these terms in a sense significant to themselves; but as regards the actual applications of these causes their statements are beyond our comprehension. For if it is for pleasure that the Gods partake of them, the nectar and ambrosia are in no sense causes of their existence; but if it is to support life, how can Gods who require nourishment be eternal?
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle analyzes the mythological claim that Gods must consume nectar and ambrosia to maintain their immortality. This implies a rule in that mythological framework where even divine beings are subject to a material requirement (nourishment) to avoid the loss of their status (mortality).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to a very specific, small group of entities (divine beings) within the cosmologies Aristotle is critiquing.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to total universal knowledge
proof: [§261] Hence it follows on his theory that God, the most blessed being, is less wise than the others, since He does not know all the elements; for He has no Strife in Him, and knowledge is of like by like: By earth (he says) we earth perceive, by water water, By air bright air, by fire consuming fire, Love too by love, and strife by grievous strife.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Based on Empedocles' internal rules as analyzed by Aristotle, the highest divine being is excluded from knowing 'Strife' because he does not possess it. In a system where 'knowledge is of like by like,' the absence of a quality results in a concrete cognitive deficit/exclusion.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule specifically targets the 'most blessed being' or the divine singular in Empedocles' system.

extracted_rules.resource: Status as a constituent of reality
proof: [§277] At any rate he says From which grew all that was and is and shall be In time to come: the trees, and men and women, The beasts and birds and water-nurtured fish, And the long-living Gods.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle quotes Empedocles to illustrate the types of beings generated by first principles. By explicitly listing 'women' alongside 'men' and 'Gods' as part of what 'is and shall be', the text confirms the inclusion of women as a fundamental, recognized category of human existence within the natural and social order.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the universal category of all women in the described world.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from mortality
proof: [§273] The school of Hesiod, and all the cosmologists... say that whatever did not taste of the nectar and ambrosia became mortal—clearly using these terms in a sense significant to themselves
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a customary rule found in the works of Hesiod and other cosmologists where immortality (protection from death) is contingent upon access to specific material resources (nectar and ambrosia). This establishes a biological stratification where one group (Gods) maintains their status through exclusive consumption.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule applies only to the small, elite group of 'Gods' or those with access to divine substances.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to comprehensive knowledge
proof: [§278] Hence it follows on his theory that God, the most blessed being, is less wise than the others, since He does not know all the elements; for He has no Strife in Him, and knowledge is of like by like
extracted_rules.reasoning: Under the theory of Empedocles discussed by Aristotle, a specific being ('God') is deprived of a resource (comprehensive knowledge/wisdom) because his nature excludes a specific element (Strife). This represents a systematic loss of a cognitive resource based on inherent status.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This refers specifically to the 'most blessed being' or a singular divine entity.

extracted_rules.resource: Reputation for wisdom
proof: [§300] This is why the vulgar and the earlier thinkers supposed that substance and Being are Body, and everything else the modifications of Body; and hence also that the first principles of bodies are the first principles of existing things; whereas later thinkers with a greater reputation for wisdom supposed that substance and Being are numbers.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle distinguishes between the 'vulgar' (common people/earlier thinkers) and 'later thinkers' based on their metaphysical theories. Those who moved away from materialism toward mathematical substances are explicitly granted a 'greater reputation for wisdom,' showing how intellectual alignment with specific philosophical schools conferred social and academic standing in 4th-century BCE Greece.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to a specific sub-section of the population—intellectuals and philosophers—rather than the general citizenry.

extracted_rules.resource: Reputation for wisdom

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be recognized as a living member
proof: [§808] A finger cannot in every state be a part of a living animal; for the dead finger has only the name in common with the living one.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a categorical exclusion where a deceased person or their parts lose their identity and biological/social standing, retaining only the 'name' but not the essence or rights of the living entity.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to every individual upon death.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be defined by unique attributes
proof: [§808] But when we come to the concrete thing, e.g. this circle—which is a particular individual, either sensible or intelligible... of these individuals there is no definition; we apprehend them by intelligence or perception; and when they have passed from the sphere of actuality it is uncertain whether they exist or not, but they are always spoken of and apprehended by the universal formula.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In Aristotelian logic and societal categorization, individuals (like 'Socrates' or 'Callias') are not subject to essential definition; they are categorized only by the group (universal) they belong to, denying them unique formal standing in the 'formula' of knowledge.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to every concrete individual in society.

extracted_rules.resource: Status as a human part
proof: [§840] for it is not the hand in any condition that is a part of a man, but only when it can perform its function, and so has life in it. Without life in it it is not a part.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues that a body part (and by extension the human subject) is only defined by its capacity to perform its natural function. A part that cannot function is 'not a part' except in name, implying that those without functional biological capacity lose the status of being part of the 'living creature' in a formal sense.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any individual with a non-functioning limb or organ, which is a significant but specific subset of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Possibility of existence without physical parts
proof: [§839] And the analogy in the case of the living thing which the younger Socrates used to state is not a good one; for it leads one away from the truth, and makes one suppose that it is possible for a man to exist without his parts, as a circle does without the bronze. But the case is not similar; for the animal is sensible and cannot be defined without motion, and hence not unless its parts are in some definite condition
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle rejects the idea that a human can be defined or exist as a purely abstract form (like a mathematical circle). He establishes a rule of 'material necessity'—that human status is tied to the physical body and its motion.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the entire human species as a definition of their existence.

extracted_rules.resource: Legal and essential definition as two-footed
proof: [§849] E.g., in the case of man, two-footed animal; for let us take this as the formula of man. Why, then, is this a unity and not a plurality, animal and two-footed?
[§850] E.g., in the case of man, two-footed animal; for let us take this as the formula of man.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The definition applies to all human beings within the society's conceptual and legal framework.

extracted_rules.resource: Identity defined by soul over matter
proof: [§846] there is a formula in accordance with the primary substance, e.g., in the case of a man, the formula of the soul; because the substance is the indwelling form, of which and of the matter the so called concrete substance is composed.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies the soul as the 'primary substance' of a man. In a Greek context, the capacity for the 'formula of the soul' (rationality) is the requirement for legal agency and citizenship, distinguishing the human from mere biological matter.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a universal biological/philosophical definition for the entire group 'men'.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to scientific definition and demonstration
proof: [§882] For this reason also there is no definition or demonstration of particular sensible substances, because they contain matter whose nature is such that it can both exist and not exist. Hence all the individual instances of them are perishable. . . then clearly there can be neither definition nor demonstration of individual sensible substances.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle establishes a rule of logic and science: individual entities (using Socrates and Cleon as examples) cannot be the subjects of scientific demonstration or formal definition. This excludes the unique, material individual from the 'resource' of being captured by higher-order scientific knowledge.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: I chose 2 because this rule applies to every individual person and sensible object in the society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to independent legal or substantial existence
proof: [§893] Hence it is clear that no universal exists in separation apart from its particulars. . . It is clear, then, both that no universal term is substance and that no substance is composed of substances.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues that a universal (the 'one-over-many') does not have a separate existence or status as a 'substance' apart from the individuals it describes. This effectively denies the 'universal' a standing independent of the particulars.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: I chose 1 because this is a foundational ontological rule that governs how all categories and groups are understood in this philosophical system.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to separate substantial existence
proof: [§893] It is obvious that even of those things which are thought to be substances the majority are potentialities; both the parts of living things (for none of them has a separate substantial existence; and when they are separated, although they still exist, they exist as matter)
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle defines the parts of living beings (arms, legs, etc.) as lacking independent existence; they only 'exist' fully when integrated into the whole. This is a rule governing the hierarchy of entities where the part is denied the status of the whole.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: I chose 3 because it specifically targets biological components within the broader category of substances.

extracted_rules.resource: Status as the moving cause of generation
proof: [§914] What is the material cause of a man? The menses. What is the moving cause? The semen.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies the male contribution (semen) as the 'moving cause' or active principle of human generation, while the female contribution (menses) is categorized as mere 'matter' or the 'potential' human. This reflects a societal and philosophical stratification where the male is granted the status of the active, defining agent in the creation of life and lineage.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule defines the fundamental roles of men and women in the process of reproduction for the entire society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to define products as independent substances
proof: [§914] But whether the substances of perishable things are separable or not is not yet at all clear; only it is clear that this is impossible in some cases, i.e. in the case of all things which cannot exist apart from the particular instances; e.g. house or implement. Probably, then, neither these things themselves, nor anything else which is not naturally composed, are substances; for their nature is the only substance which one can assume in the case of perishable things.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle distinguishes between 'natural substances' and 'implements' or 'houses' (man-made objects). By denying the status of 'substance' to the products of human craft (technical labor), the text reinforces a hierarchy where the output of the artisan class is considered ontologically inferior to natural or biological entities.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those engaged in manufacturing and construction, a distinct segment of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be defined by actuality (soul)
proof: [§914] whether animal means a soul in a body or a soul; for the soul is the substance and actuality of some body. The term animal would be applicable to both cases; not as being defined by one formula, but as relating to one concept.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the context of Greek thought and law, defining 'animal' (which includes humans) by the 'soul' (actuality) rather than just the body establishes a legal and philosophical framework for agency. Being defined by 'actuality' is a prerequisite for having rights or being a holder of 'essence' in a civic sense.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This definition encompasses all living beings within the philosopher's scope, the most general level possible.

extracted_rules.resource: Status as a natural substance
proof: [§917] Those about which there is agreement are natural substances: e.g. fire, earth, water, air and all the other simple bodies; next, plants and their parts, and animals and the parts of animals; and finally the sensible universe and its parts
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies animals and plants as universally agreed-upon 'substances' (ousia). In the context of Greek thought, being a substance is the primary requirement for having a 'nature' and being a subject of scientific and legal consideration, as opposed to mere accidents or artificial products.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all biological entities, a massive category within the natural world.

extracted_rules.resource: Status as independent substance
proof: [§930] No one of them, nor the combination of any two of them, is substance, but nevertheless each one of them contains something analogous to substance. And just as in the case of substances that which is predicated of the matter is the actuality itself, so in the other kinds of definition it is the nearest approximation to actuality. E.g., if we have to define a threshold, we shall call it a piece of wood or stone placed in such-and-such a way; and we should define a house as bricks and timber arranged in such-and-such a way
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle distinguishes between natural substances (like animals) and man-made objects (thresholds, houses, chests). He explicitly states that the configurations of man-made objects are not 'substances' but only 'analogous to substance.' This creates a hierarchy where the products of human labor have an inferior ontological and legal status compared to natural entities.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the broad category of all manufactured goods and structures.

extracted_rules.resource: Status as the moving cause of generation
proof: [§948] E.g., what is the material cause of a man? The menses. What is the moving cause? The semen. What is the formal cause? The essence. What is the final cause? The end.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle assigns the role of the 'moving cause' (the active, formative principle) exclusively to the male semen, while the female contribution (menses) is relegated to the 'material cause' (the passive substrate). In Aristotelian teleology, being the moving cause is a higher functional status than being the material cause.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule describes the fundamental biological and social understanding of the roles of all men and women in the process of human generation.

extracted_rules.resource: Status as the moving or formal cause of offspring
proof: [§948] E.g., what is the material cause of a man? The menses. What is the moving cause? The semen. What is the formal cause? The essence. What is the final cause? The end.

extracted_rules.resource: Direct identification with the rational essence
proof: [§935] Soul and essence of soul are the same, but man and essence of man are not, unless the soul is also to be called man; and although this is so in one sense, it is not so in another.

This distinction grounds his political theory where only those who live by the 'soul' (rationality) are full citizens, while 'man' as a biological composite includes material needs that can be subordinated.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This technical definition underpins the legal and philosophical status of all human beings within the Aristotelian framework.

extracted_rules.resource: Status as moving cause of generation
proof: [§953] E.g., what is the material cause of a man? The menses. What is the moving cause? The semen. What is the formal cause? The essence.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies the male contribution (semen) as the 'moving cause'—the active principle that initiates development and provides the form—while the female contribution (menses) is relegated to the 'material cause' or passive substrate. This reflects a biological and legal hierarchy where the male is the primary agent of creation.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes a general biological/social rule regarding the roles of the sexes in generation, affecting the entire adult population.

extracted_rules.resource: Status as moving cause of generation
proof: [§953] E.g., what is the material cause of a man? The menses. What is the moving cause? The semen.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This principle applies to all women in the context of human reproduction and the definition of 'man'.

extracted_rules.resource: Power to produce contrary results
proof: [§973] ...the scientific man can produce both contrary results. For the rational account includes both, though not in the same way; and it is in the soul, which contains a principle of motion, and will therefore, by means of the same principle, set both processes in motion, by linking them with the same rational account.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle distinguishes between irrational potencies (like heat) which can only produce one result, and the 'scientific man' who, possessing a rational formula (art/science), has the specific capacity to direct that power toward opposite outcomes (e.g., a doctor producing health or disease).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those trained in 'arts' or 'productive sciences', a distinct but significant professional class in Greek society.

extracted_rules.resource: Capacity for productive choice
proof: [§973] Since some of these principles are inherent in inanimate things, and others in animate things and in the soul and in the rational part of the soul, it is clear that some of the potencies also will be irrational and some rational. ... Every rational potency admits equally of contrary results, but irrational potencies admit of one result only.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle establishes a rule of natural/social order where inanimate things are restricted to single, necessary causal paths, whereas rational agents (living beings with souls) have access to choice and 'contrary results'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a very broad categorical distinction between all inanimate objects and all rational beings.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to professional status during inactivity
proof: [§973] Obviously a man will not be a builder unless he is building, because to be a builder is to be capable of building; and the same will be true of the other arts. If, therefore, it is impossible to possess these arts without learning them at some time and having grasped them, and impossible not to possess them without having lost them at some time... when the artist ceases to practice his art, he will not possess it; and if he immediately starts building again, how will he have re-acquired the art?
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues against the Megaric school to defend the societal rule that a professional (like a builder) retains their status and capability (potentiality) even when not actively working. This protects the social and economic standing of craftsmen.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all skilled 'artists' or craftsmen, a specific segment of the free population.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of action through desire
proof: [§973] Therefore there must be some other deciding factor, by which I mean desire or conscious choice. For whichever of two things an animal desires decisively it will do, when it is in circumstances appropriate to the potency and meets with that which admits of being acted upon.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle defines the mechanism by which animate beings (animals/humans) exercise their capabilities. It establishes that action is a result of internal 'desire or conscious choice' rather than external compulsion, provided the environment is appropriate.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies broadly to all living creatures with 'souls'.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to skill through exercise
proof: [§973] Since all potencies are either innate, like the senses, or acquired by practice, like flute-playing, or by study, as in the arts, some—such as are acquired by practice or a rational formula—we can only possess when we have first exercised them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a rule of social/technical acquisition: to gain the resource of an 'art' (economic and social standing), the individual must have the right to exercise that skill before they are fully proficient.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any individual undergoing apprenticeship or education in the arts.

extracted_rules.resource: Capability to produce contrary results
proof: [§988] And since contrary attributes cannot be induced in the same subject, and science is a potency which depends upon the possession of a rational formula, and the soul contains a principle of motion, it follows that whereas the salutary can only produce health, and the calefactory only heat, and the frigorific only cold, the scientific man can produce both contrary results.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle distinguishes the 'scientific man' (the expert or artist) from irrational entities by their unique capacity to produce both a result and its opposite (e.g., health and disease) through the possession of a rational account (logos). This grants a specific group a higher order of agency and causal power compared to those lacking such training.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule applies specifically to those possessing 'productive sciences' or 'arts', such as doctors or master builders, which is a significant but distinct subset of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Capability to produce contrary results
proof: [§984] Every rational potency admits equally of contrary results, but irrational potencies admit of one result only. E.g., heat can only produce heat, but medical science can produce disease and health.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle defines a category of agents (those with irrational potencies, which include inanimate things and those without the 'rational part of the soul' in a specific context) as being restricted to only one possible outcome of their action, unlike the rational scientist.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes a broad category including all non-rational agents and inanimate objects, affecting a large portion of the 'actors' in the Greek ontological world view.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be recognized as a practitioner of an art
proof: [§991] Obviously a man will not be a builder unless he is building, because to be a builder is to be capable of building; and the same will be true of the other arts. If, therefore, it is impossible to possess these arts without learning them at some time and having grasped them, and impossible not to possess them without having lost them at some time (through forgetfulness or some affection or the lapse of time; not, of course, through the destruction of the object of the art, because it exists always), when the artist ceases to practice his art, he will not possess it.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes the social and cognitive requirements for holding a professional identity (a 'builder' or 'artist'). In Greek society, the possession of a 'techne' (art/skill) was a prerequisite for specific economic and social roles, and Aristotle outlines that this possession is predicated on prior learning and continuous capability.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the broad class of skilled laborers and professionals (artisans) in the Greek polis.

extracted_rules.resource: Possession of rational or practiced potencies
proof: [§1009] Since all potencies are either innate, like the senses, or acquired by practice, like flute-playing, or by study, as in the arts, some—such as are acquired by practice or a rational formula—we can only possess when we have first exercised them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule for the acquisition of 'potencies' (capabilities) associated with specific occupations like flute-playing or other arts. It dictates that these rights/skills are only validly possessed after the individual has undergone a period of exercise/practice.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: It targets any individual seeking to enter a skilled profession or 'techne'.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from being classified as blind or deaf
proof: [§996] If, then, that is blind which has not sight, though it would naturally have it, and when it would naturally have it, and while it still exists, the same people will be blind many times a day; and deaf too.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues against the Megaric view by asserting that a person's status (such as being sighted or hearing) is a persistent potentiality, not just an active state. In a legal and social sense, being classified as 'blind' or 'deaf' carried specific social and physical implications; Aristotle's rule preserves the status of the able-bodied even when not actively using their senses.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to nearly all individuals who are not biologically disabled.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be called a scholar

This indicates that professional or intellectual status is a recognized resource held even during periods of inactivity.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific subset of the educated population (scholars) but describes a general rule within that class.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to personal will in healing
proof: [§1027] The definition of that which as a result of thought comes, from existing potentially, to exist actually, is that, when it has been willed, if no external influence hinders it, it comes to pass; and the condition in the case of the patient, i.e. in the person who is being healed, is that nothing in him should hinder the process.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While the context is metaphysical, Aristotle uses the real-world example of medical treatment ('the person who is being healed') to define the move from potential to actual. He identifies 'will' (the patient's or doctor's) as a necessary condition for the concrete civic process of healing to occur, implying that healing is a process dependent on the state and volition of the individual.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any individual in a state of illness seeking treatment, which is a broad category.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be recognized by professional title
proof: [§1033] Hence it seems impossible that a man can be a builder if he has never built, or a harpist if he has never played a harp; because he who learns to play the harp learns by playing it, and similarly in all other cases.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all skilled laborers and performers, a significant but specific segment of the free population.

proof: [§1040] But since something of that which is being generated is already generated, and something of that which is being moved as a whole is already moved (this is demonstrated in our discussion on Motion), presumably the learner too must possess something of the science.

proof: [§1091] Protagoras says that man is the measure of all things, meaning, as it were, the scholar or the man of perception; and these because they possess, the one knowledge, and the other perception, which we hold to be the measures of objects.

extracted_rules.resource: Recognition of natural attribute possession

This implies that certain social or legal capacities (attributes) are systematically withheld from individuals based on their age category until they reach a specific threshold.

In a society where legal and social privileges (like appearing in court or holding office) often depend on a reputation for 'goodness' (arete), the existence of an intermediate category suggests a group that lacks the definitive standing of the 'virtuous' elite but is not yet classified as 'bad' (kakos).

extracted_rules.resource: Status as a number

extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly states that being 'one' or a 'unit' is not sufficient to be categorized as a 'number' if the entity is indivisible. In his ontology, a number must be a plurality measurable by a unit; therefore, the most basic indivisible units are excluded from the category of 'number'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to a specific ontological category (indivisible units) within the broader context of mathematical and physical entities discussed in Greek science.

extracted_rules.resource: Classification as many/plurality
proof: [§1145] since fewness is constituted not by one, as some hold, but by two.

extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle defines plurality and number such that the 'unit' (one) is the measure of the many, but is not itself 'many' or a 'plurality'. Since plurality starts at two, the 'one' is systematically excluded from the benefits/attributes of being a plural group.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a foundational logic/mathematical rule that affects how all discrete units are categorized in this system of thought.

extracted_rules.resource: Categorization by natural genus
proof: [§1169] Thus it is evident that in relation to what is called genus no species is either the same or other in species (and this is as it should be, for the matter is disclosed by negation, and the genus is the matter of that of which it is predicated as genus; not in the sense in which we speak of the genus or clan of the Heraclidae, but as we speak of a genus in nature); nor yet in relation to things which are not in the same genus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle distinguishes between a 'genus in nature' (scientific/logical category) and a 'genus or clan' (social lineage like the Heraclidae). He explicitly excludes social lineages from the rules of natural differentiation and species-definition, meaning those in a clan do not receive the same categorical 'standing' in his metaphysical system as natural entities.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to specific aristocratic or historical lineages (like the Heraclidae) within Greek society, rather than the entire population.

extracted_rules.resource: Status as same species as men
proof: [§1170] The question might be raised as to why woman does not differ in species from man, seeing that female is contrary to male, and difference is contrariety; and why a female and a male animal are not other in species, although this difference belongs to animal per se, and not as whiteness or blackness does; male and female belong to it qua animal. [...] Male and female are attributes peculiar to the animal, but not in virtue of its substance; they ar material or physical.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues that gender (male vs. female) is a material modification rather than a formal or substantial difference. By concluding that women do not differ in species from men, he affirms their fundamental membership in the same category of 'man' or 'animal,' even if he views the difference as a material 'modification.'
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the most general categorical level of human existence, affecting the entire female population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be defined by substance rather than matter
proof: [§1176] Male and female are attributes peculiar to the animal, but not in virtue of its substance; they ar material or physical. Hence the same semen may, as the result of some modification, become either female or male.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle clarifies that being female is not a matter of 'substance' (the essential formula) but a 'material or physical' attribute resulting from a 'modification' of semen. This categorizes the group's defining characteristic as an accidental or material variation rather than a core human essence.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This definition covers all women in contrast to all men.

extracted_rules.resource: Status as same species as others regardless of color
proof: [§1173] Hence the whiteness or blackness of a man does not produce this, nor is there any specific difference between a white man and a black man; not even if one term is assigned to each. For we are now regarding man as matter, and matter does not produce difference; and for this reason, too, individual men are not species of man, although the flesh and bones of which this and that man consist are different.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While applying to all humans, it specifically addresses the distinction between different physical types (white/black).

extracted_rules.resource: Legal/academic standing as a practitioner of truth
proof: [§1183] Therefore it remains that the philosopher is the man who studies the things which we have described, in so far as they are Being. [...] Plato was not far wrong in saying that the sophist spends his time in the study of unreality.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly excludes Sophists (and to an extent dialecticians) from the highest form of science (Philosophy/Wisdom). He categorizes their work as dealing with 'unreality' or 'accidental' being, thereby denying them the right to be recognized as practitioners of the primary science of Being within the established intellectual hierarchy.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in communal discussion

He concludes there is no 'arguing' with them, essentially stripping them of their standing as participants in the community of reason.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule targets a specific philosophical type or school of thought rather than a broad demographic, but has wide implications for who is heard in the 'agora' of ideas.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to serve as a measure of truth/reality
proof: [§1183] But to attach equal importance to the opinions and impressions of opposing parties is foolish, because clearly one side or the other must be wrong. Such being the case, the one party should be taken as the measure, and the other not.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to investigate immovable or non-physical matters
proof: [§1192] It is not the province of physics, because the whole business of the physicist is with things which contain in themselves a principle of motion and rest; nor yet of the science which inquires into demonstration and scientific knowledge, for it is simply this sort of thing which forms the subject of its inquiry.

This effectively bars practitioners of physics from claiming authority or 'province' over the study of mathematical objects or the first principles of demonstration.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule targets a specific professional/intellectual class (physicists) rather than the general population, though this class is a significant category in the context of Greek intellectual labor.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to define universal principles

extracted_rules.resource: Right to study Being qua Being
